{"step_by_step_thinking": "To determine the best method for detecting occlusal clearance of an onlay, we evaluate the available options based on clinical relevance and documentation. Option A, depth cuts, is not specifically mentioned in the documents as a method for detecting occlusal clearance. Option B, visual inspection, is limited in accuracy because it does not provide objective data on clearance, especially in hidden or subtle areas. Option C, wax chew-in, is a technique used to simulate occlusal contact and can help identify high spots or interferences, but it is not specifically referenced in the documents as the best method for detecting occlusal clearance. Option D, articulating paper, is directly mentioned in Document [28] (UMLS source) as a method used to assess static and dynamic occlusion, and to detect occlusal trauma or high spots after dental restoration. This makes articulating paper the most accurate and clinically validated method for detecting occlusal clearance.
